ground_truth.title: What do you guys think will be the trend in the watch market in 2024?
ground_truth.author: sanmartinwatch
ground_truth.main_content:
sanmartinwatch
Jan 14, 2024

With 2023 being the year that vintage style was popular and many Swiss brands gradually releasing smaller sized watches, what do you guys think will be the trend in the watch market in 2024?

Babylon Timer
Jan 15, 2024

In-house movements.

countingseconds
Jan 15, 2024

Same as 2023. I don't expect anything new and exciting to be the trend.

cristianTWL
Jan 15, 2024

This

KainX
Jan 15, 2024

Rolex Daytona and AP Royal Oak.
Come on, do you really have to ask?

ruffy
Jan 15, 2024

There's a chance the bottom could fall out of the market and used models will become reasonable again. Not likely but one can always hope.

Cosmodromedary
Jan 16, 2024

I'm predicting a continuation and evolution of current trends, with no sudden surprises.

- 36 to 40mm as the fashionable range of sizes, and continued emphasis on slim + high WR.
- Continuing shift towards cleaner designs, more GADA, less overt complication. I think this is why GMT has replaced Chronograph as the most popular complication among young professionals, it doesn't look as try-hard but is still "something more".
- Oversized and "iced" bling will still exist, but only for a very particular market, mainly Gen X and older (immensely uncool among the new generation of watch nerds).
- Vintage will still be popular, but we'll see an increasing shift towards the retro quartz era (70s, 80s and 90s) as opposed to mechanical vintage (50s and 60s).
- Synthwave retro-futurism will ramp up, including more upmarket digital watches with more interesting backlight colours. Digital GMT (world time) may become a big thing again.
- More high quality quartz watches in general, using better quartz movements, including more sweeping seconds hands (Rondas that don't hit the second markers are just not good enough and the market doesn't want cheap nasty quartz anymore)
- Integrated bracelets will be more common as a short sharp trend that many brands will jump on board with.
- The upmarket brands race will be to produce a luxury watch in purple gold, a new type of gold that is very difficult to produce, with aluminium as the alloying agent.
- Once the above milestone is hit, heat coloured cases may see brief popularity, as a cheaper (but still expensive) option to scratch that new luxury kitsch itch.
- In general we'll see more interesting use of colour, like San Martin are already diving into, but fewer pastels, less tiffany blue, and more 80s colours like cyan, magenta and lime.

KPU1012
Jan 16, 2024

I have been out of watches for about five years (Apple watch...) and what attracts me and I am hoping continues are slim, clean designs with good water resistance. Would also like to see expanded use of the PT5000 movement. The two San Martins I have purchased are a 39mm pilot with 200m WR and a 37mm GADA with 100m WR. Both of these feel so much better than the 44mm x 14mm divers in my watch box.... Expect these trends to continue Including smaller, slimmer divers.

chippewa
Jan 16, 2024

Really hoping fauxtina lume dies as a trend

Tavarea
Jan 16, 2024

Slimmer watches with good specs using miyota 9000 series and eta clone movements. I also could see the peacock sl4801 being used for a new wave of automatic chronos from Chinese manufacturers.

mconlonx
Jan 18, 2024

"Midsize" will stabilize at 38mm; 36mm will still be considered "small."

I used to be in a minority, but I am also frequently ahead of trends - always lobbied for thinner watches, and am willing to sacrifice WR meters to do it, but the dynamic water pressure brigade seems to have a 100m limit as far as what they prefer. 30-50m does it for me, especially if it shaves a few mm off an otherwise decent design.

Yellow will be the hot color.

People will rediscover that a lot of what we think pass as dress watches now, are actually sport watches, and GADA will swing dressier - less lume and the reintroduction of alpha-style hands.

More retro-releases as companies raid their back catalogs (and those of others...) for ideas and inspiration.

Wishes:
San Martin and microbrands start using more unabashedly Made in China movements - PT5000, ST17xx, Tongji - and proudly print "Made in China" on the dial.

Chinese-made traveler GMT movement at same quality level as PT5000

AardnoldArrdvark
Jan 18, 2024

mconlonx said:
San Martin and microbrands start using more unabashedly Made in China movements - PT5000, ST17xx, Tongji - and proudly print "Made in China" on the dial.

I'd prefer '中国制造' or 'Zhōngguó zhìzào' to 'Made in China' in English but otherwise agree with all you posted.
